I have been a licensed driver in Manila since 1983. We moved to Negros Island in 2011. Between 2011 and the present, the roadscape in Manila has drastically changed. And my greatest fear now is how to maneuver through the roads I once knew.  We now have lanes that go between pillars under the skyway whose labels are not  visible or presented to the driver several meters before he reaches a potential lane change. That could mean a charge of abrupt swerving or reckless driving for me.

Rules can be justly applied if the driving environment is efficient and supports compliance from drivers. Why not put up large, visible road signages instead of arbitrary enforcers who are tempted to use their discretion as a source of 'pampadulas'. 

There is a notorious spot under the curving skyway near Terminal 3, Newport World Hotels and Villanor Airbase. Arbitrary enforcers will flag you for using an inner lane when you turn left going to the Highway. No signs whatsoever. Almost all the Grab drivers I've spoken to are familiar with that spot not because it is clearly labeled but because they have experienced the notoriety of the enforcers.

One thing is for sure-- the calendar days is highly unfair.  When you require servicing processes from govt offices, they count "business days" and not "calendar days" .  We all know that govt could just as easily suspend work for any excuse, while we all toil still despite typhoon signals or disasters.

Hi Morning Coffee Thoughts 2.0 , I just wanted some clarification sa timeline ng issue na ito. Una po, sa original post ni Mr. Deakin, yung mga dates were not specific, sinabi lang po nya yung timing ng mga naganap by saying "This happens a few days before Christmas," "they are closed on Saturday for license retrieval." 
The only time na may nabanggit sya na date was when he said that they were told to "come back on the 5th" (January 2026). Now if the apprehension happened on December 18, and they were given 15 days to settle, does that mean that they have Dec 19 to Jan 2 para pumunta sa LTO? I am asking this kasi sa pagkakaintindi ko based sa statement ni Mr. Deakin, lampas sa 15 day period na sila nagpunta sa LTO at pumunta sila ng January 3 na. Sa statement kasi ni Mr. Deakin sinabi nya na pumunta sila sa LTO East Ave ng Saturday pero closed sila for license retrieval at nagtanong sila kung kailan bukas at sinabihan sila na bumalik ng January 5. Yung mga dates ng Saturday between Dec 18 to Jan 5 ay Dec 20, 27, and Jan 3. Parang hindi naman sila masasabihan na bumalik ng Jan 5 kung Dec 20 or 27 sila nag punta. And posible na Jan 3 sila nag punta sa LTO dahil they were under the assumption that it would be 15 business day dahil sinabi din nya sa statement nya "You were closed for 8 days out of those 15. Surely it's 15 working days?"

Grabe naman yung essay mo, parang nag-defend ka ng thesis tungkol sa "The Art of Political Half-Bakedness" 😅 But seriously, ang ganda ng structure ng critique mo — methodical, data-driven, pero ramdam mo yung genuine frustration ng isang citizen na hindi lang nag-rant para mag-rant.

Ang pinakamatindi sa analysis mo is yung unprogrammed funds part. Kasi diba, it's like sinabi ng doctor na "may cancer ka," tapos yung treatment plan is tinanggal lang yung kalahati ng tumor kasi "mahirap naman alisin lahat." Sir, if the Supreme Court justice is practically waving a constitutional red flag, bakit kailangan pa ng token veto? It's giving "I want credit for addressing the problem without fully solving it because fully solving it would cost me politically." 

At yung "half-baked" observation mo? Chef's kiss. 👨‍🍳 Kasi totoo — it's the consistent inconsistency that kills trust. Parang every move is calculated to do just enough to deflect criticism but never enough to actually burn bridges with the system that keeps him afloat. It's political self-preservation dressed up as reform.

Pero alam mo yung nakakalungkot? Yung last part mo — yung disclaimer. The fact that you have to say "this is not a call for resignation because mas kadiri pa yung alternatives" is perhaps the most damning indictment of our current political reality. We're essentially trapped in a "lesser evil" framework na parang Hunger Games pero walang Katniss, lahat si Peeta — good at surviving, bad at actual fighting. 

And honestly, yung prediction mo about the lame duck final years? Hindi siya hot take, it's just... math. Walang re-election, shrinking political capital, rising disapproval — ano na lang ang incentive to take real risks? Wala. So we get exactly what you described: a long, awkward stretch ng "paano ba matapos 'to."

Pero ang tanong ko lang: if surveys continue this trajectory and his net trust stays negative or gets worse, does he even care anymore? Kasi minsan ang scary thought is baka the strategy is just to run out the clock, maintain just enough support sa key allies, and exit stage left with whatever wealth and connections were secured along the way. 

Yung problema kasi sa half-baked presidents is they rarely wake up one day at mag-decide na mag-fully bake. The oven's already set to a comfortable mediocre temperature, and changing that setting requires more courage than keeping it where it is.

Anyway, salamat sa thoughtful read. This is the kind of political commentary we need more of — hindi puro galit lang, pero galit with receipts. 📊

FROM GIFT TO BETRAYAL
(An Open Letter to the Department of Education – Sorsogon City Division)

I write this letter not only as a donor but also as a father, a son, and a citizen who believes in the power of education to change lives.

In 2020, I donated a parcel of land, covered by TCT No. 083-2020000239, consisting of 2,701 square meters situated in Barangay Guinlajon, Sorsogon City. This was not an ordinary donation. It carried with it a very clear and personal intention—that the property be used solely for the actual, direct, and exclusive benefit of GUINLAJON ELEMENTARY SCHOOL.

I envisioned classrooms rising from that land, playgrounds where children could laugh, and learning spaces where young minds could dream of a brighter future.

This was also my way of honoring the vision of my late father, Congressman Vicente Leyva Peralta, who believed deeply in providing our youth with a healthy and proper environment for learning. I wanted to continue his legacy by helping relieve overcrowded classrooms and giving our children a chance to learn with dignity.

But early this year, I visited the property and was heartbroken by what I saw. Instead of classrooms for children, the property had become the site for the Division Office of DepEd SDO Sorsogon City.

I stood there gutted, disappointed, and angered. What was meant for those who have little was turned into a building for those who already have much. This was never my intention, and it will never be.

Even more disheartening is the simultaneous construction of two Division Offices in Sorsogon—one in Cabid-an, Sorsogon City and another in Guinlajon, Sorsogon City. This duplication is not merely wasteful, while learners are left to endure overcrowded classrooms, dilapidated facilities, and a shortage of resources, public funds and efforts are diverted toward redundant offices that serve bureaucracy rather than the children they are sworn to protect
The conditions of the Deed of Donation were clear and unequivocal: the land was to be used exclusively for Guinlajon Elementary School and its educational activities. To use it otherwise is a gross violation of trust and a betrayal of the generosity that I, and many other Filipinos, extend in the hope of uplifting our children.

What saddens me most is not only the misuse of my donation, but the deep mistrust it sows.

Donations are acts of faith. They represent trust in institutions and leaders to honor the will of the giver. When that trust is broken, the loss goes beyond one piece of land—it threatens the very foundation of generosity in our schools. Future donors may hesitate. Others may withhold their help. And in the end, it is the children—the very learners DepEd is sworn to protect—who will suffer the consequences of that mistrust.

Every donation made to education carries with it a story of sacrifice and hope—whether it comes from a wealthy patron or from someone who gives despite their hardships.

If people see that their gifts are misused or diverted from their intended purpose, what assurance will remain for future generosity? Who will still have the heart to give if trust is broken?

What does it mean to be Makadiyos if the promises made for our children are broken?

What value is there in calling ourselves Makatao if the needs of learners are set aside for the comfort of administrators?

And how can we ever be truly Makabansa if we betray the very spirit of bayanihan and generosity that defines us as Filipinos?

Are the core values—Makadiyos, Makatao, Makakalikasan, and Makabansa—expected only from students? Certainly not. DepEd personnel are not exempt from these values; in fact, they must embody them even more. They are the mirror through which learners see what is right and just.
This issue is not mine alone. It speaks to something larger: THE CREDIBILITY OF THE DEPARTMENT OF EDUCATION AND ITS OFFICIALS.

I therefore call on the DepEd SDO Sorsogon City to restore the property’s intended use for the GUINLAJON ELEMENTARY SCHOOL.

I appeal to the public as well: let us not allow acts of goodwill to be undermined by negligence or misplaced priorities. Our children are the rightful beneficiaries of every donation given in the name of education.

“THE BEGINNING AND THE END OF EDUCATION IS THE LEARNER, NOT ANYTHING OR ANYONE ELSE”

A painful truth we rarely confront 🇵🇭

Filipinos are talented. Hard-working. World-class when given the chance.
Yet as a nation, we keep going in circles.

Why?

Because historically, our biggest weakness has not been the Filipino worker — it has been Filipino leadership.

Time and again, those entrusted with power prioritize:
 • themselves,
 • their families,
 • their inner circles,

instead of institutions, systems, and long-term national good.

We blame colonial mentality, corruption, even poverty — but the pattern is consistent:
personal interest first, country second (if at all).

A sobering case in point: Philippine Airlines (PAL).

For years, our flagship carrier struggled — bloated systems, weak execution, recurring losses.
Then something remarkable happened.

A foreign CEO was hired.
No political debt. No family dynasty to protect. No local favors to repay.

The result?
 • Clear accountability
 • Professional discipline
 • Operational focus
 • Measurable improvement

Suddenly, PAL began to recover.

Not because Filipinos suddenly became capable —
but because leadership finally became professional instead of personal.

This is the hard lesson:

A country cannot transform if leadership is treated as inheritance instead of responsibility.

Until we demand leaders who:
 • build institutions, not empires
 • think in decades, not election cycles
 • serve the nation, not surnames

we will keep exporting our best people —
and importing the leadership discipline we refuse to cultivate.

This isn’t anti-Filipino.
It’s pro-Philippines.

And real love for country starts with telling the truth.

150.text:
Every country protects its own interest. That is the first rule of geopolitics. The stronger the economy, the greater the influence; the weaker the economy, the faster the need to align with a protector.
After World War II, the world did not divide itself because of ideology—it divided itself because of power. The U.S. and the former Soviet Union became superpowers, and countries aligned not because they loved democracy or communism, but because survival demanded it.
For nearly a century, the United States has acted as the world’s dominant power. It became the “global police” not because it was morally perfect, but because it controlled trade routes, financial systems, and the world’s reserve currency. Most countries accepted this arrangement because it worked.
But let’s be honest: the United States has never acted purely to protect democracy. It has intervened, supported wars, overthrown leaders, and violated sovereignty when its economic or strategic interests were threatened—from Vietnam and Korea to Iraq, Afghanistan, Libya, and now Venezuela.
The recent U.S. action in Venezuela should be viewed through this same realist lens. Officially, it is about law enforcement and narco-terrorism. Strategically, it is about reasserting dominance in the Western Hemisphere, weakening China and Russia’s foothold, and influencing global energy flows. Oil still matters—not as the sole reason, but as a constant strategic advantage.
Yes, this violates sovereignty. But so does Russia’s invasion of Ukraine, driven by fear of NATO expansion. And so does China’s bullying in the West Philippine Sea, where it tests limits, applies pressure slowly, and turns economic dependence into political leverage.
The difference is not intention—it is method and scale.
China does not invade outright. It infiltrates. It finances. It builds dependence. It supports authoritarian leaders quietly and waits. Countries like North Korea, Cuba, Venezuela, and parts of Africa did not fall under China’s influence overnight; they were absorbed through economic necessity and political convenience.
The Philippines itself could have followed that path—had these activities not been exposed.
So the real question is uncomfortable but unavoidable:
If the world must be policed, who do we prefer holding the baton?
The United States is imperfect, intrusive, and often hypocritical—but it still operates within a system that allows pushback, alliances, and global participation. China offers peace talks and development, but always with leverage attached. Russia relies on force and revisionism.
That is why, despite all reservations, I still view the U.S. action in Venezuela as a signal the world understands: the existing order—flawed as it is—is still being enforced. China and Russia may challenge it, but they are not yet ready to replace it.
In geopolitics, morality rarely wins. Stability does. And right now, the world still runs on a system the United States controls.

That being said my opinion is that, the Filipinos are not matured enough for democratic government. While it is good to hear that it equates to freedom - freedom itself requires a lot of learning, a lot of responsibility, a lot of respect towards each others rights and a lot of maturity which we as Filipinos still lack of. I always think it has to do with how we are educated and how we are raised. While there is so much time used to learn subjects like Philippine history and the likes - these subjects are meant to learn what traspired then and to some extent idolized some people. This type of learning is what we become. There is not much subjects to learn community responsibility, political responsibility and the like. 

I am living in Switzerland and I think they have the most democratic government. Kids learn responsibility, civic duties and political duties as part of their school curriculum. As an example, as early as kindergarten I could see how they are made aware of how to treat the forest lovingly, literallt going there often enough to build relationship with the nature - which turns them into nature loving people and as they grow up they become its protectors- it turns into as simple as not seeing garbages or litters here. 

Their years of learning rewards them - Ordinary people could propose a referendum, launch popular initiatives, and even challenge laws. People vote up to four times a year both at communal, federal and cantonal level. Economic and political topics such as raising taxes, building new roads are all voted by the people. Likewise, the topics you could hear from ordinary people here are not about politicians but about real issues. The real power really lies with the people who were educated on how the system functions - since they are young. Likewise, there is no "real president" as the presidency is just chosen from a 7-member federal council who rotates annually so there is no real concentration of power. So compared to all other democratically strong nations, I think Switzerland have the most powerful citizens.

So while I believe that Filipinos are strong and of good natured, I do not think that many people are matured and responsible enough to hold freedom in their hands - mainly because we are not educated to do so.

A significant victory of the coalesced groups came during the elections for delegates to the 1971 Constitutional Convention; about one-third of the delegate seats were won by progressives and independents who later formed a potent bloc to push through sweeping and fundamental structural changes in the government and society... Of this political transformation taking place in the Philippines, Fr. Bruno Hicks, an American Franciscan missionary deported later by the martial regime who now lives in Stockton, CA wrote:
"I saw more and more Filipinos getting into the political arena. I saw simple and conscientious peasants forming their own political groups, expressing themselves, beginning to vote independently of their landlords and employers." Father Hicks then asks: "Could this have been the reason why martial law was declared: because democracy was just beginning to work [and] the grievances of the masses were finally getting organized, getting aired, bringing pressure to bear on the political institutions?"

Educating voters will require a long term plan, and short term ones. Both will require political will from the government, and participation from the public sector.

The  long term involves education per se. The goal should be to get the masses to think critically. Pretty much how the middle class does. Without it, patronage politics will continue to thrive, and elections will remain a popularity contest.

Short term will involve a number of things, and would have to be continuous. It can start with a house to house voter education. That can include everything you mentioned in real action to helping people enlighten themselves about the truth. 

It’s a lot of work. And, once momentum slows down, that’s it. And it can’t be done without, first, recognizing how troubled we are. Because, as a democracy, we are at the mercy of the masses. And, for as long as they lack in their abilities to decipher truth from hype, and believe their “destiny” is sealed, we can’t expect them to save us.

Yes, there is a them and us. But this is not our doing. The divide and conquer has always been within the administration’s control. So, before we can get into the process of fixing the system, we first need to put somebody up there who shares the same vision. But how, when we are dominated by uncritical voters? It’s like the chicken and the egg. But I’m open to suggestions.

If this practice has existed for many years and a lawmaker sincerely believes it is wrong, the logical and responsible first step is correction, not spectacle. Congress is not just a place to expose problems. It is the institution empowered to fix them. Publicly insinuating wrongdoing without immediately advancing concrete reforms weakens the institution more than it strengthens accountability.

It is also important to separate two issues that are being blurred. First, large allocations of public funds are not automatically corrupt. Second, the absence of transparency and enforceable liquidation standards is where abuse becomes possible. The problem is not only the amount. The problem is opacity. If these funds were clearly itemized, strictly liquidated, published in full and made accessible to taxpayers in real time, the risk of abuse would be significantly reduced. That is a policy failure that Congress itself has the authority to correct.

If a lawmaker believes break allowances or lump sum MOOE releases are being used improperly, the proper response is to file measures that mandate disclosure, tighten liquidation requirements, remove certification only liquidations and impose penalties for misuse. Those are concrete actions. 

Posting allegations online without a parallel legislative proposal turns a governance issue into a narrative battle rather than an institutional reform effort.

There is also a question of responsibility. Maligning the House as a whole while remaining part of it, without offering a clear corrective mechanism, erodes public trust without giving the public a solution.

Transparency should be institutionalized, not personalized. Reform should be structural, not reactive. If the system is flawed, then fix the system using the tools of lawmaking, not implication.

In the end, lawmakers are elected to strengthen the bureaucracy, not to weaken it through selective exposure. Accountability that leads to reform is valuable. Outrage without a roadmap is not. If this practice is truly problematic, the remedy lies in legislation, clear rules, open books, and sustained oversight, not in politicized disclosure that leaves the structure exactly as it was.

Ah, Happy New Year sa lahat! 2026 na, pero parang 1896 pa rin ang drama sa Philippine politics—same colonial vibes, but with filters and livestreams. Solid yung post mo, bro/sis! It hits hard on Rizal's "A la Juventud Filipina," where he wasn't just hyping up any kabataan, but the ones with brains, balls (or ovaries), and a backbone for real change. Hindi yung mga "pag-asa ng bayan" na feeling nila, ang hope ay nasa likes at shares, not sa substance.

Deep dive: Spot on sa critique kay "Meow Congressman" (haha, imagine Rizal seeing that—baka sabihin niya, "Noli Me Tangere, pero with cat ears?"). His viral antics scream Gen Z rebellion, pero when it blurs into spectacle over strategy, it's like ordering revolution sa TikTok: entertaining, but does it deliver? Then si Leviste, the "anti-politician" with a silver spoon—exposing bribes sounds heroic, pero selective outrage? That's just old-school trapo tactics in millennial packaging. Parang sinasabi, "Corruption bad, unless it's my ally's project—then it's just 'business as usual.'" Tangina, if this is the sample, talo nga tayo, pero humor me: Baka next, we'll have a senator doing ASMR sessions on pork barrel. "Shhh, eto ang gentle whisper ng kickbacks..."

But seriously, your point on qualifiers is gold—Rizal meant educated, principled youth, not influencers chasing clout. Sa 2026, we need more of those "boring" heroes: yung nag-aaral ng bills sa committee, hindi nagpo-pose sa feed. Voters like us? We're the curators—huwag maging sucker sa shiny wrappers. Let's vote for the ones na may receipts, not just receipts from shopping sprees sa dynasty funds.

Cheers to a hopeful year ahead, where kabataan steps up for real. Sana hindi tayo mag-end up with more warnings from history. Maligayang Bagong Taon ulit—may your resolutions include spotting fake pag-asa from a mile away! 🎉
